sibling_claims.P1: Schizophrenia is not marked off from ordinary life by the presence of hallucinations or strange beliefs — those are common — but by their persistence, conviction, and cost; the diagnostic line is real, yet it is a threshold on a continuum, not a wall.
sibling_claims.P2: The symptoms that define schizophrenia in the public mind — voices and delusions — are the ones that matter least for how a life actually goes; the negative and cognitive symptoms nobody pictures are what predict whether someone works, studies, and connects.
sibling_claims.P3: The 'split mind' folk-model is the exact inverse of the mechanism — this is a failure of integration, not a multiplication of selves.
sibling_claims.P4: The most powerful things that help in schizophrenia are the least-discussed — coordinated care and family support rival medication for preventing relapse — and 'non-adherence' is usually a rational response to real side-effect burden, not a failure of will.

sources.title: Peer-professional first-person account: schizophrenia from the inside--phenomenology and the integration of causes and meanings.
sources.finding: A psychologist who himself became psychotic narrates his own recovery and the integration of medication, insight therapy and a changed social world.
sources.verbatim_quote: I stepped off that vehicle later in West London with a radically different and, as far as I am concerned, 'corrected' brain from the one I had when I stepped on it.

sources.title: First Person Account From a Person in Jail With Schizoaffective Disorder.
sources.finding: First-person narrative of living with schizoaffective disorder while incarcerated — fear, voices, daily exhaustion. Alternate/backup FPA for P5.
sources.verbatim_quote: It is exhausting to live like that and always be looking around and be prepared.

sources.title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of recovery in schizophrenia.
sources.finding: Pooling 50 studies, ~1 in 7 (13.5%) met a combined clinical+social recovery criterion sustained >=2 years — and the rate has not improved over decades. CONTESTED (see Lally 2017).

numbers.what_it_is: median proportion meeting combined clinical+social recovery criteria
numbers.abstract_sentence: The median proportion (25%-75% quantiles) who met our recovery criteria was 13.5% (8.1%-20.0%).
Based on the best available data, approximately, 1 in 7 individuals with schizophrenia met our criteria for recovery.
Despite major changes in treatment options in recent decades, the proportion of recovered cases has not increased.

sources.contested_note: CONTESTED — strict criterion, chronic samples; first-episode cohorts report higher. Report the range.

sources.title: Remission and recovery from first-episode psychosis in adults: systematic review and meta-analysis of long-term outcome studies.
sources.finding: In first-episode psychosis cohorts, pooled remission was 58% and recovery 38% — far higher than chronic-sample estimates — and outcomes did not progressively deteriorate.

numbers.what_it_is: pooled remission rate in first-episode psychosis
numbers.abstract_sentence: The pooled rate of remission among 12301 individuals with FEP was 58% (60 studies, mean follow-up 5.5 years).

numbers.what_it_is: pooled recovery prevalence in first-episode psychosis
numbers.abstract_sentence: The pooled prevalence of recovery among 9642 individuals with FEP was 38% (35 studies, mean follow-up 7.2 years).

sources.contested_note: This IS the contest source. Pair explicitly with Jaaskelainen 2013.

sources.title: Supported employment for people with severe mental illness: systematic review and meta-analysis of the international evidence.
sources.finding: IPS supported employment more than doubles competitive-employment rates vs traditional vocational rehabilitation, regardless of local economy.

numbers.what_it_is: pooled RR for competitive employment, IPS vs traditional vocational rehab
numbers.abstract_sentence: The overall pooled risk ratio for competitive employment using IPS compared with traditional vocational rehabilitation was 2.40 (95% CI 1.99-2.90).

sources.title: The effectiveness of supported employment for people with severe mental illness: a randomised controlled trial.
sources.finding: EQOLISE RCT (6 European centres, n=312): 55% of IPS patients worked at least one day vs 28% with standard vocational services.

numbers.what_it_is: proportion working at least 1 day: IPS vs traditional vocational services
numbers.abstract_sentence: 85 (55%) patients assigned to IPS working for at least 1 day compared with 43 (28%) patients assigned to vocational services (difference 26.9%, 95% CI 16.4-37.4).

sources.title: The population impact of severe mental illness on violent crime.
sources.finding: Swedish national registers: only ~5% of violent crime is attributable to severe mental illness — patients commit roughly 1 in 20 violent crimes.

numbers.what_it_is: population-attributable risk fraction of SMI for violent crime
numbers.abstract_sentence: Overall, the population-attributable risk fraction of patients was 5%, suggesting that patients with severe mental illness commit one in 20 violent crimes.

sources.contested_note: Anti-stigma correction. Absolute risk small even where relative risk elevated; most risk concentrated with substance misuse.

sources.title: Perpetration of violence, violent victimization, and severe mental illness: balancing public health concerns.
sources.finding: People with severe mental illness are more often victims of violence than perpetrators; victimization is the greater public-health concern.

numbers.what_it_is: prevalence of perpetrating vs being violently victimized (outpatients)
numbers.abstract_sentence: Among outpatients, 2% to 13% had perpetrated violence in the past six months to three years, compared with 20% to 34% who had been violently victimized.

sources.title: Years of potential life lost and life expectancy in schizophrenia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
sources.finding: Schizophrenia associated with ~14.5 years of potential life lost and life expectancy ~64.7 years; the gap has not narrowed over time.

numbers.what_it_is: weighted-average years of potential life lost
numbers.abstract_sentence: Schizophrenia was associated with a weighted average of 14.5 years of potential life lost (95% CI 11.2-17.8), and was higher for men than women.
The overall weighted average life expectancy was 64.7 years (95% CI 61.1-71.3), and was lower for men than women.

sources.title: Prevalence, incidence and mortality from cardiovascular disease in patients with pooled and specific severe mental illness: a large-scale meta-analysis of 3,211,768 patients and 113,383,368 controls.
sources.finding: Severe mental illness carries ~10% CVD prevalence and substantially raised hazard of CVD death (HR 1.85), driven partly by antipsychotic use and obesity.

numbers.what_it_is: hazard ratio for CVD-related death, SMI vs controls
numbers.abstract_sentence: The incidence was also higher for ... CVD-related death (HR=1.85, 95% CI: 1.53-2.24).

numbers.what_it_is: pooled CVD prevalence in SMI patients
numbers.abstract_sentence: The pooled CVD prevalence in SMI patients (mean age 50 years) was 9.9% (95% CI: 7.4-13.3).

sources.title: Premature Mortality Among Adults With Schizophrenia in the United States.
sources.finding: US cohort (N=1,138,853): schizophrenia carries 3.7x all-cause mortality; CVD is the single leading cause; accidents kill more than twice as many as suicide.

numbers.what_it_is: standardized mortality ratio vs general population
numbers.abstract_sentence: Adults with schizophrenia were more than 3.5 times (all-cause SMR, 3.7; 95% CI, 3.7-3.7) as likely to die in the follow-up period as were adults in the general population.
Accidental deaths (119.7 per 100,000 person-years) accounted for more than twice as many deaths as suicide (52.0 per 100,000 person-years).

sources.contested_note: Corrects assumption that suicide is the main killer.

sources.title: The social determinants of mental health and disorder: evidence, prevention and recommendations.
sources.finding: World Psychiatry review mapping causal evidence that structural social determinants (poverty, marginalization, disadvantage) shape mental-health outcomes and recovery.

numbers.what_it_is: causal link between social determinants and outcomes
numbers.abstract_sentence: we first map out the literature that supports a causal link between social determinants and later mental health outcomes.

sources.contested_note: Framing source only — qualitative claim, not a numeric statistic.

sources.title: Caregiver burden and the associated factors in the family caregivers of patients with schizophrenia.
sources.finding: Among 215 family caregivers, 38.2% perceived severe burden, predicted by income, job loss from caregiving, illness duration and caregiving frequency.

numbers.what_it_is: proportion of family caregivers perceiving severe burden (Zarit)
numbers.abstract_sentence: Family caregivers of patients with schizophrenia reported a high level of caregiving burden, with 38.2% of the caregivers perceiving severe burden relating to their role.

sources.contested_note: Single-country cross-sectional; illustrative, not global prevalence. Pair with Peng 2022.

sources.title: Disease-Related Risk Factors for Caregiver Burden among Family Caregivers of Persons with Schizophrenia: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
sources.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
sources.finding: Meta-analysis of 34 studies: heavier caregiver burden is significantly associated with more severe symptoms, greater psychopathology, worse functioning and longer illness duration.

numbers.abstract_sentence: a significant association between a heavier burden and disease-related risk factors, including more severe symptoms, greater general psychopathology, greater severity of functional impairment, and longer duration of illness.
